skill.name: projection-patterns
skill.description: Build read models, materialized views, and projection rebuild workflows for event-sourced systems.
skill.summary: Guide CQRS projection design, checkpoints, rebuilds, and query-focused read models.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and network indicators. Review found these are documentation false positives from Markdown fences, normal SQL examples, descriptive words, and reference links. No prompt injection, executable payload, credential access, or data exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported external command locations are Markdown code fences or inline file references. They do not invoke a shell or execute user-controlled input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched characters are Markdown formatting and inline resource paths. There is no command runner, shell API, or executable script in these locations.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Links Flagged as Network Use
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are reference links to external learning resources. The skill does not contain runtime network calls.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both findings are Markdown resource links. They do not send data, fetch remote code, or perform network activity during skill use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Descriptive Text Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits occur in words such as description and design. No cryptographic function or algorithm selection appears in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are normal prose or event projection field names. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or cryptographic API usage is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQL Identifiers Flagged as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance hits are SQL WHERE clauses and event data identifiers in projection examples. They do not inspect the host system.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are database update filters inside illustrative projection code. There are no commands that collect system identity, users, files, or environment details.

content.value_statement: Event-sourced systems need fast read models without fragile duplication. This skill guides projection design, checkpoints, rebuilds, and query-focused materialized views.
actual_capabilities: Explains live, catchup, persistent, and inline projection styles.
Provides Python examples for projection runners and event handlers.
Shows read model updates for order, product, sales, and customer views.
Covers checkpoint storage, replay, and rebuild workflows.
Highlights projection practices for idempotency, transactions, lag monitoring, and ordering.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete production framework.
Examples focus on Python, async PostgreSQL, and Elasticsearch patterns.
It does not choose database schemas without domain requirements.
It does not include deployment automation or operational runbooks.

use_cases.title: Design CQRS Read Models
use_cases.description: Plan materialized views that serve query needs from event streams.

use_cases.title: Rebuild Projections Safely
use_cases.description: Define checkpoint and replay flows for recovery, migrations, and data corrections.

use_cases.title: Create Search and Reporting Views
use_cases.description: Map domain events into search indexes, summaries, and aggregate tables.
use_cases.target_user: Data product teams adding query features

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Projection Type
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a new read model
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a projection type for this event-sourced feature. My events are: [events]. My query needs are: [queries].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Read Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a projection read model for [domain]. Include handled events, target tables or indexes, checkpoint strategy, and rebuild considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Review Projection Reliability
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this projection design for idempotency, ordering, transaction boundaries, checkpoint safety, and replay behavior: [design].

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Migration Rebuild
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a projection rebuild plan for changing [old read model] to [new read model]. Include backfill, validation, cutover, rollback, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: Design a projection for order summaries from order lifecycle events.
output: Read model fields for status, totals, dates, and counts.
Validation checks for totals and lifecycle transitions.

output_examples.input: Review a projection that updates several customer tables.
output: Idempotency risks and duplicate event handling guidance.
Operational checks for projection lag and rebuild status.

output_examples.input: Plan a product search projection from product events.
output: Create, update, price change, and delete handling.
Monitoring points for failed writes and stale documents.

best_practices: Start from concrete query requirements before choosing tables or indexes.
Make handlers idempotent so events can be replayed safely.
Store checkpoints and monitor projection lag in production.
anti_patterns: Do not couple separate projections through shared hidden state.
Do not skip error handling for failed event application.
Do not build one normalized read model for every query.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps design and review read-side projections for CQRS and event-sourced systems.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: It provides patterns and examples. Production code still needs testing, observability, and domain-specific integration.

faq.question: Which databases does it cover?
faq.answer: The examples use PostgreSQL-style read models and Elasticsearch-style search indexes.

faq.question: Can it help with rebuilding projections?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers checkpoints, replay, rebuild planning, and validation after reconstruction.

faq.question: Is it limited to order systems?
faq.answer: No. Order, product, sales, and customer examples show patterns that can be adapted to other domains.

faq.question: Does it run commands or connect to services?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill is documentation with examples and reference links.
